runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity of single mothers in the U.S.: the percent change in employment/labor-force participation from a 1% increase in the net-of-tax wage.
citations: Eissa and Liebman (1996) on labor supply responses of single mothers to tax/earnings incentives
Moffitt (2002/2003) summaries on welfare reform and labor supply elasticities
Blundell and MaCurdy (1999) on wage elasticity and labor supply modeling/elasticity magnitudes
runs.reasoningSummary: I expect a positive but modest medium-run elasticity: higher net-of-tax wages typically increase single mothers’ participation, but frictions (child care, job matching, fixed costs) and already-high baseline participation limit the response, yielding a distribution centered around ~0.1 with plausible tails from near-zero to a few tenths.

runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity of single mothers with respect to the net-of-tax wage (percent change in employment or labor-force participation per 1% change in net-of-tax wage).

runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity of single mothers in the United States: the percent change in employment or labor-force participation from a 1% increase in the net-of-tax wage.
citations: Standard labor supply findings: net-of-wage increases raise labor-force participation/employment for women with elasticities typically modest but positive
Single-mother labor supply evidence from US quasi-experimental work (tax credits/earnings exemptions) often implying small-to-moderate participation responses
Broader household labor supply models suggesting participation elasticities for secondary earners are larger than intensive-margin elasticities but still well below one in many settings
runs.reasoningSummary: I expect a positive but relatively moderate medium-run elasticity: higher net wages increase participation, though constraints (childcare, imperfect job matching, and non-wage factors) likely limit the responsiveness. Hence the median around 0.2 with a plausible range from small effects to moderately larger effects.

runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity of single mothers in the United States: the percent change in employment/labor-force participation from a 1% change in the net-of-tax wage.

runs.reasoningSummary: I endorse a modest positive medium-run elasticity: participation responses are typically nontrivial but constrained by caregiving, health, job matching, and existing labor supply behavior, leading to a median around 0.25 with limited mass near zero and a tail toward somewhat larger effects.
